title-group.article-title: Combinatorial Probabilities of Multiple Fragmentation Events Explain Polypeptide MS/MS Intensity Distribution, Overrepresentation of Smaller Fragments, and Missing Middle of Top‐Down MS

article-meta.aff: 1 Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Northeastern University, Boston, USA

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: Lyon and coworkers demonstrated that multiple random fragmentation events can bias intensity distributions toward smaller terminal fragment ions. With any high‐yield method of dissociation, such as high‐energy collisional activation, this phenomenon can greatly influence the intensity distribution of product ions in MS/MS spectra. Previously, multiple fragmentation events were simulated with computationally intensive stochastic studies. The goal of this work is to provide a mathematical model that explains the results obtained from computational stochastic studies.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We present a probabilistic model that predicts terminal and internal product ion intensities based on polypeptide size and the number of fragmentation events. This model is validated by demonstrating convergence with the previous stochastic model and described that the intensity trend from smaller fragments to larger fragments follows the probability of multiple fragmentation events on peptide backbone.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Under the stated assumptions, the analytical expressions formally demonstrate that the “missing middle” is a necessary mathematical consequence of multiple fragmentation events, and they reproduce the stochastic simulation results with exact agreement while reducing computation time from hours to less than 1 s. Our method consistently offers greater accuracy and mathematically proves the mechanistic necessity of “missing middle” phenomenon in top‐down MS. The abundances of fragments from stochastic simulation distribute around the average value calculated by the probability formula within twofold coefficient variance (%CV).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The closed‐form probability expressions accurately describe the combinatorial consequences of multiple fragmentation events under the stated assumptions. Because they are computationally inexpensive and differentiable, these expressions could be integrated with sequence‐dependent fragmentation propensities in future machine learning models for MS/MS spectral prediction.
Keywords: CID, fragmentation, internal ion, probability analysis, top‐down MS
Revised 2026 Apr 30; Received 2026 Mar 10; Accepted 2026 May 4; Issue date 2026 Aug 30.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: This work aims at closing the gap between the interpretation and the prediction of MS/MS spectra, which often occurs independently. Both the bottom‐up and top‐down MS/MS (TDMS) communities often interpret the relative intensities of peaks MS/MS spectra in terms of fragmentation propensities [1], which relate to the statistical frequency of fragmentation between pairs of amino acid residues. Fragmentation propensities are determined based upon large, assigned spectral libraries, and are largest for “XP‐” and “DX‐” type cleavages. High propensity cleavages are well characterized. For example, work pertaining to the mobile proton hypothesis and proline (XP) effect, and localized (acidic) protons for DX cleavages, are mechanistically understood to the quantum mechanical level [2, 3]. While fragmentation propensity helps the post hoc interpretation of MS/MS spectra, it fails to account for well‐known positional effects, and we are unaware of its implementation in predicting MS/MS spectra.

On the other end of the spectrum are spectral predictors that function well but are mechanistically opaque. In protein mass analysis, the ability to simulate MS/MS spectra has received considerable attention, because it is required for de novo generation of spectral libraries, because there is limited top‐down spectra library, e.g., for data dependent analysis (DDA) and data independent analysis (DIA) workflows [4]. The most accurate means we are aware of are data‐driven artificial intelligence models developed to enable high‐accuracy simulation of MS/MS spectra from given sequences of protein and peptide [4, 5]. These techniques all rely on the existing data. Here, the low sequence coverage of large polypeptides, which is due in part to a lack of assigned internal fragment ions in these data sets, led to limited ability of predicting the relative abundance of fragments produced from large proteins [6].

This study is driven by a phenomenon widely reported in TDMS community—more fragment ions are observed near the termini than in the middle portion of the protein. This phenomenon has been termed the “missing middle” and confounds proteoform ID and the ability to generate sequence information from the interior portion of proteins. Specifically, the gap length in sequence space between two adjacent fragments increases with the length of terminal fragments. The missing middle was originally attributed to decreased exposure of protein's interior portions to collisional activation. In this “ball of spaghetti,” proteins are folded in the gas phase such that only the terminal amino acids are available for cleavage [7]. Lyon and coworkers recently provided an additional interpretation for this phenomenon by showing that random multiple fragmentation events are a novel contributor to the relative intensities of MS/MS spectra [8]. Their studies implied that missing middle is an inherent feature of any protein that undergoes more than one fragmentation event. Specifically, multiple fragmentation events diminish the signal intensities of relatively large, i.e., interior, fragments. Eventually, the chance of detecting longer terminal fragments diminishes to the point that the b‐ or y‐ion fragments near the center of the protein sequence are not detected. Using stochastic simulations of ubiquitin, they determined the relative intensities of both terminal and internal fragments as a function of the number fragmentation events per protein. It takes only two random fragmentation events per protein to produce a significant bias toward higher MS signal intensity for smaller terminal fragment ions, and this phenomenon becomes progressively more pronounced upon additional fragmentation. These in silico stochastic results provided a qualitative explanation for the long‐observed, analogous skew in experimental TDMS spectra, as well as a qualitative relationship for the relative intensity of terminal and internal fragment ions.

Whereas previous stochastic simulations provided qualitative insight into these relationships, the present work makes four specific advances within the same set of simplifying assumptions. First, we derive closed‐form combinatorial expressions (Equations 1–3) for the probability of every possible terminal and internal fragment, replacing Monte Carlo sampling with an exact analytical solution. Second, these expressions constitute a formal proof that, under the stated assumptions, the relative probability of observing a terminal fragment decreases monotonically with fragment length for any number of cleavage events n ≥ 2 (see Equation 1, where P(bi) is a decreasing function of i); this makes the “missing middle” an inevitable mathematical consequence of multiple fragmentation rather than an empirical observation requiring separate explanation. Third, the analytical solution is computationally orders of magnitude faster than stochastic simulation (< 1 s vs. > 16 h for ubiquitin at n = 4 cleavage events), enabling its practical use as a component in spectral prediction pipelines. Fourth, because the probability is now an explicit, differentiable function of peptide length and cleavage number, it can, in principle, be incorporated as a prior or regularization term in machine learning models that also account for sequence‐dependent fragmentation propensities—although such integration remains future work and is not demonstrated here.

sec.title: Modeling the Random Dissociation Events on Peptide Backbone
sec.p: In collision‐induced dissociation (CID) of a peptide with a size of N amino acids in the mass spectrometer, there are N‐1 sites of amide bonds that can be broken to generate fragments. Lyon and colleagues [8] made the following assumptions to simplify the random process of CID: (1) The number of dissociation events (n) on every peptide molecule is the same; (2) dissociation at any amide bonds would not impact the probability of dissociation event on any other sites, meaning that the dissociation event of any site of the peptide is independent from each other; and (3) dissociation events can happen to every site with equal probability.

These assumptions define an idealized model in which all cleavage sites are treated as equivalent. In reality, cleavage propensities depend on amino acid identity, charge location, activation energy, and gas‐phase structure. The present work deliberately isolates the combinatorial component of multiple fragmentation from these sequence‐dependent effects, so that the contribution of random cleavage statistics to the intensity distribution can be quantified independently. The extent to which real fragmentation departs from this null model decreases as the number of fragmentation events increases (because high probability cleavages tend to happen before lower probability). This can be assessed in future work by comparing model predictions with experimental fragment intensities, ideally in the context of training machine learning to account for multiple fragmentation events and sequence‐dependent fragmentation propensity.

sec.title:
The Probability of Generating Each b‐ and y‐Ion With a Length of i and k Amino Acids (b
i and y
k )
sec.p:
Scheme 1 visibly shows the derivation of combinatorial probability equations. With assumptions above, when there are n times of dissociation events happening in N‐1 possible sites, the number of possible combinations of dissociation site is N−1n=N−1!n!N−1−n!. Among all the possible combinations of dissociation sites, those ones that generate b
i ions must include the i th amino acid on the peptide chain from N‐terminus, and other n‐1 sites must be behind the i th amide bond. The number of dissociation sites left to be selected will be N‐i‐1, so the number of possible combinations of selection that can generate b
i ions is N−i−1n−1=N−i−1!n−1!N−i−1−n−1!=N−i−1!n−1!N−i−n!. The probability of generating b
i ion can be calculated with the combination number of selecting n‐1 sites to dissociate from the N‐i‐1 sites on the rest of the peptide chain N−i−1n−1 divided by the total number of possible dissociation site combinations N−1n.

caption.p: Derivation of combinatorial probability model with the example of a 10‐AA peptide.

sec.p:
Derivation of the probability function for y
k ion is the same, but the index k starts from C‐terminus towards N‐terminus.

sec.title: The Probability of Generating Each Internal Fragment
sec.p: To generate internal fragments from the i th to the j th amino acid, the (i‐1)th and the j th amide bond must be cleaved without any other dissociation site between them. So, the probabilities of generating each internal fragment would be the combination number of selecting n‐2 sites to dissociate from N‐(i‐j)‐2 sites on both sides of the internal fragment of the interest divided by the total number of combinations N−1n when n is larger than 2; when n equals 2, the probability of generating each internal fragment are equally 1N−1n; when there is only 1 cleavage (n = 1), there will not be internal fragments generated.

sec.title: Validating the Equation by Comparing the Calculated Average Frequency of Fragments to Stochastic Simulation Results
sec.p: In this study, ChatGPT was used to code a python program that calculates the expected frequencies of fragment ions. This program was used to reproduce Lyon and colleagues' stochastic study to provide a comparison to our probabilistic model. Specifically, their study involved simulated breaking a starting group of 1 million peptides, a given number of cleavages, and repeating this 20 times to yield the distribution of frequency of each fragment around an average frequency. And because our probability model was developed to describe this stochastic study with explicit equations, the average frequency of each product fragment calculated from these equations would be the same as that of simulated results from the stochastic method. The program is shared in the Supporting Information.

sec.title: Statistical Comparison of Analytical and Stochastic Results
sec.p: The coefficient of variation (%CV) was computed for each fragment as %CV = (σ/μ) × 100%, where σ is the standard deviation of observed fragment frequencies across 20 independent stochastic simulation runs, and μ is the average frequency observed from the stochastic study (Equations 1–3). Percentage residuals for each fragment were calculated as follows:
If the probability equations are not correct, the distribution of %Residuals is going to be biased and fall out of the range of 2% CV to one side.

sec.title: Evaluating the Probability Equations With a Stochastic Study
sec.p: The probability model can be applied to calculate the probability of each single fragment and, when the probability is multiplied with the total number of peptide molecules, the expected frequencies of all the possible fragments. The most straightforward way to evaluate if the probability model describes this random process well is comparing the observed frequencies in a stochastic study that simulates the cleavage of certain number of peptides to the calculated frequencies.

Our probability model produced qualitatively identical results to stochastic simulation (Figure 1). Specifically, the stochastic simulation is slower and less accurate, but with sufficient repeats converges on our probability model (Figure 2). The matching distribution patterns of b‐, y‐, and internal fragments mean that the probability model developed here describes the same fragmentation phenomenon as previous stochastic simulation. With overlapping distribution patterns, statistical analysis was carried out to calculate the %CV of stochastic study results compared to the calculated results from probability formulas, as presented in Figures 2 and 3 for b‐ions and internal fragments, respectively, with the boundary of two‐fold %CV. Here, %CV (coefficient of variation, defined as σ/μ × 100%) quantifies the dispersion of the stochastic simulation results around the simulation average. The factor of 2 is used as a descriptive tolerance band; for a normal distribution, approximately 95% of observations fall within ±2 standard deviations of the mean, so ±2 × %CV provides an approximate envelope.

caption.p: The ubiquitin MS/MS fragment abundances calculated by the probabilistic model recapitulates stochastic simulations. Representation of statistical fragmentation pattern of ubiquitin in 1, 2, 3, and 4 fragmentation patterns between avg. simulation of stochastic model for 20 times and numerical model. The frequencies are in log scale over mass (Da) in the scatter plot graph for terminal b‐ions and internal fragment ions, respectively. Given the sum of difference between the two models is 0, the two models converge on the same values.

The probabilistic model provides the spectral simulation of terminal fragments by calculating the average abundance of fragments in multiple fragmentation events. Representation of the statistical comparison plot between expected b‐ion frequencies and average observed fragment frequencies from a 20‐observation stochastic study that simulated cleavage of ubiquitin for 1 million times (number of simulations = 1 million). Left: the frequency distribution of fragments of corresponding mass from 1 to 4 fragments. Right: black dots represent %Residual of the simulation results calculated by %Residual = (Simulated Frequency – Calculated Mean Frequency) / Calculated Mean Frequency × 100%. Orange line indicates ±2% CV, within which more than 95% of the %Residual between simulated frequencies and expected frequencies calculated from Equations (1)–(3) fall, confirming acceptable convergence of the stochastic model. %CV = (σ/μ) × 100%, where σ is the standard deviation of observed fragment frequencies across 20 independent simulations.

The probabilistic model provides the spectral simulation of internal fragments by calculating the average abundance of fragments in multiple fragmentation events. Representation of the statistical comparison plot between expected internal fragment frequencies and average observed fragment frequencies from a 20‐observation stochastic study that simulated cleavage of ubiquitin for 1 million times (number of simulations = 1 million). Left: the frequency distribution of fragments of corresponding mass from 1 to 4 fragments. Right: black dots represent %Residual of the simulation results calculated by %Residual = (Simulated Frequency – Calculated Mean Frequency) / Calculated Mean Frequency × 100%. Orange line indicates ±2% CV, within which more than 95% of the %Residual between simulated frequencies and expected frequencies calculated from Equations (1)–(3) fall, confirming acceptable convergence of the stochastic model. %CV = (σ/μ) × 100%, where σ is the standard deviation of observed fragment frequencies across 20 independent simulations.

sec.title: Computational Efficiency of the Probability Model Compared to Stochastic Simulation
sec.p: In the results above, the increasing trend in %CV is a result of decreased frequency because the frequency value in the in silico simulation is a discrete parameter. The linear regression of %CV of simulation results (Figure 4) reveals a linear relationship between log (%CV) and log (Frequency) following the linear equation.

caption.p: The linear relationship of %CV and frequency. The logarithm of %CV of the internal fragment frequencies generated from repetitively inducing four fragments to a million of ubiquitin sequences for 20 times is linearly related to the logarithm of frequencies, following the linear regression function logCV=−0.5logFrequency+2.

sec.p: The same derivation is applicable to get 1% CV.

These expressions indicate that the greater number of amino acids and cleavage there is, the larger number of simulations are needed to confidently simulate the level of fragment frequencies and the intensity distribution. Table S1 showed the number of simulations and time needed to simulate n‐fragmentation on protein of N amino acids with %CV under 1% and 5%. The example used in this study, ubiquitin, a 76‐amino‐acid protein, will need about 500 000 000 simulations of a 4‐cleavage in silico experiment. On an 8‐core‐CPU computer running the simulation program provided in this study, it will take 50 min to finish one group of in silico experiments, and more than 16 h to finish simulating 20 groups of observations. However, with the expressions and the program provided in this study, it takes less than 1 s to calculate the relative intensities of each fragment. Table S1 provided a checklist of the #simulation and simulation time needed for different lengths of peptides.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, we develop a probability model with mathematical expressions to describe the random process of protein fragmentation, together with an efficient python code that can simulate the relative intensity of ion. Assumptions include a fixed number of fragmentation and independent, equal probability of cleavage on each amide bond. The manual and explanation of the code package is attached to the Supporting Information. This probability model quantitatively describes how multiple fragmentation events lead to the diminishing likelihood of observing the middle part of large proteins in top‐down MS. Importantly, Lyon et al. [8] showed that the stochastic simulation of random multiple fragmentation reproduces experimentally observed terminal fragment intensity trends for ubiquitin under UVPD and HCD activation. Because our analytical model yields exact numerical agreement with that same stochastic simulation (Figures 1, 2, 3), the experimental support reported by Lyon et al. extends to the closed‐form expressions derived here—at least for terminal ions. The agreement for internal fragments remains to be validated experimentally, as Lyon et al. did not separately report internal fragment intensities. We acknowledge the gap between our probability model and a working spectral predictor. We note, however, that such a closed‐form model could serve as one component of a more complete spectral predictor—for example, as a prior distribution that is modulated by experimentally determined, sequence‐dependent fragmentation propensities. We suspect that phenomena relating to subsequent fragmentation may not be incorporated into current machine learning models and therefore may require explicit integration with machine learning frameworks. While pure assumption‐constraint simulation in stochastic study is computationally heavy‐duty, the efficiency provided by the closed‐form probability equations would make it possible to integrate the polypeptide fragmentation physical model into a machine learning framework in the future.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Novera Alam: conceptualization, investigation, writing – original draft, validation, visualization. Wensheng Yang: conceptualization, investigation, writing – original draft, methodology, validation, writing – review and editing, software. Jeffrey N. Agar: conceptualization, funding acquisition, resources, writing – review and editing, methodology, validation, project administration.

caption.p: Table S1: Representation of the number of simulations and time (min) needed to get all the %CV within 1% and 5% in a stochastic simulation.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are openly available in Programs‐in‐Combinatorial‐Probability‐in‐TDMS at https://github.com/WenshYang/Programs‐in‐Combinatorial‐Probability‐in‐TDMS.git.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are openly available in Programs‐in‐Combinatorial‐Probability‐in‐TDMS at https://github.com/WenshYang/Programs‐in‐Combinatorial‐Probability‐in‐TDMS.git.
